coredata.dc:title: Regulating high specific capacitance NCS/α-MnO2 cathode and a wide potential window α-Fe2O3/rGO anode for the construction of 2.7 V for high performance aqueous asymmetric supercapacitors
coredata.dc:creator: Sajjad, Muhammad
Lu, Wen
coredata.dc:description: The long-term durability, safety, and high specific power, supercapacitors (SCs) hold unlimited promise for replacing non-renewable energy sources. Aqueous asymmetric supercapacitors (AASCs) offer numerous economic and environmental benefits. The small voltage of AASCs, however, limits their progress for large-scale industrial applications. AASCs with voltage windows (>2.0 V) have made significant progress in the last few years. Herein, a novel hierarchical α-Fe2O3 ultra-thin nanowires grown on rGO aerogel (denoted as α-Fe2O3/rGO) composite was designed as the anode. Taking advantage of the rGO aerogel protective layer, the α-Fe2O3/rGO anode reveals a prolonged voltage window up to -1.5–0 V, which considerably boosts capacitance up to (270 F g−1@1 A g−1), and (106.7 F g−1@ 10 A g−1) along with superb stability owing to the good integration of rGO network and α-Fe2O3. Moreover, NiCo2S4 nanosheets coated on α-MnO2 nanorods (denoted as NCS/α-MnO2) as a protection layer to complement the wide potential cathode, as a result, the NCS/α-MnO2 composite displays an optimized voltage window of 0–1.2 V, a high capacitance (594 F g−1@1 A g−1), and outstanding cycling stability. More importantly, the assembled NCS/α-MnO2//α-Fe2O3/rGO AASC device functions in an optimized voltage (2.7 V) by means of ultra-high specific energy of 142.1 Wh kg−1, at the specific power of 1350 W kg−1 with the downfall of the specific energy of 18.75 Wh kg−1 at the specific power of 67,500 W kg−1 with exceptional stability (79% of its initial capacitance retention). Additionally, we demonstrated a single AASC device with enough energy to light a 20 mW green LED and emitted light over 40 s, which opens up possible realistic applications. Finally, this study provides an avenue for fabricating and designing a wide voltage using a compositing technique, which seems to be one of the promising route to increase specific energy of AASCs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: AASC
Specific capacitance
Wide voltage window
Specific energy
NCS

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast primabst pubtype ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Supercapacitive performance evaluation of α-Fe2O3/rGO and NCS/α-MnO2 electrodes (three-electrode system)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD pattern of NCS/α-MnO2, and (b) XRD pattern of α-Fe2O3/rGO aerogel composite, (c) crystal structure and bonding formation between NCS and α-MnO2, (d) the attachments and structural formation of α-Fe2O3 and rGO.
XPS spectra of NCS/α-MnO2 composite, (a) Ni 2p, (b) Co 2p, (c) S 2p, and α-Fe2O3/rGO aerogel composite, (d) Mn 2p, (e) O1s, (f) Fe 2p, (g) O1s, (h) C 1 s.
TEM images, (a-d) NCS/α-MnO2, (in d, inset) is the SAED pattern of NCS/α-MnO2, (e-i) α-Fe2O3/rGO aerogel composite, and (i) is the SAED pattern of α-Fe2O3/rGO aerogel composite.

Capacitive performance evaluation of the electrodes, (a) CV curves of α-Fe2O3/rGO, and (c) CV curves of NCS/α-MnO2 electrode at different scan rates, (c) GCD curves of α-Fe2O3/rGO, and (d) GCD curves of NCS/α-MnO2 electrodes, (e) capacitance vs. current density plot, (f) stability test for 5000 cycles, respectively.

Electrochemical performance evaluation of NCS/α-MnO2//α-Fe2O3/rGO AASC device, (a) CV curves of NCS/α-MnO2 and α-Fe2O3/rGO at 50 mVs−1, (b) CV curves of the device at 5 to 2000 mVs−1, (c) GCD curves of the device at different current densities from 1 to 50 A g−1, (d) cyclic stability test for 10,000 cycles at 6 A g−1.
(a) Nyquist plots before and after cycling stability test, (inset) shows the enlarged view, (b) Bode plots before and after cycling test.
(a) Ragone plot, (b) enlarge view of the Ragone plot, (c) schematic of the electrode design and charge process of the NCS/α-MnO2 cathode, and α-Fe2O3/rGO anode in the present AASC device system.

(a) Energy diagram of rGO and α-Fe2O3, and (b) rGO and α-Fe2O3 after forming a heterojunction, (c)α-Fe2O3 surface containing free electrons accumulation, (d-f) a green LED light was illuminated by AASC device for more than 40 s.
Specific capacitance of the NCS/α-MnO2 and α-Fe2O3/rGO electrode materials was calculated from GCD measurements.
Comparison of electrochemical performance between the as-fabricated NCS/α-MnO2@α-Fe2O3/rGO AASC device and other previously reported AASCs.

head.ce:title: Regulating high specific capacitance NCS/α-MnO2 cathode and a wide potential window α-Fe2O3/rGO anode for the construction of 2.7 V for high performance aqueous asymmetric supercapacitors

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical Science and Engineering, Yunnan University, Kunming 650091, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemical Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aCollege of Chemical Science and Engineering, Yunnan University, Kunming 650091, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Energy Storage Technologies, Yunnan University, Kunming 650091, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bInstitute of Energy Storage Technologies, Yunnan University, Kunming 650091, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The long-term durability, safety, and high specific power, supercapacitors (SCs) hold unlimited promise for replacing non-renewable energy sources. Aqueous asymmetric supercapacitors (AASCs) offer numerous economic and environmental benefits. The small voltage of AASCs, however, limits their progress for large-scale industrial applications. AASCs with voltage windows (>2.0 V) have made significant progress in the last few years. Herein, a novel hierarchical α-Fe2O3 ultra-thin nanowires grown on rGO aerogel (denoted as α-Fe2O3/rGO) composite was designed as the anode. Taking advantage of the rGO aerogel protective layer, the α-Fe2O3/rGO anode reveals a prolonged voltage window up to -1.5–0 V, which considerably boosts capacitance up to (270 F g−1@1 A g−1), and (106.7 F g−1@ 10 A g−1) along with superb stability owing to the good integration of rGO network and α-Fe2O3. Moreover, NiCo2S4 nanosheets coated on α-MnO2 nanorods (denoted as NCS/α-MnO2) as a protection layer to complement the wide potential cathode, as a result, the NCS/α-MnO2 composite displays an optimized voltage window of 0–1.2 V, a high capacitance (594 F g−1@1 A g−1), and outstanding cycling stability. More importantly, the assembled NCS/α-MnO2//α-Fe2O3/rGO AASC device functions in an optimized voltage (2.7 V) by means of ultra-high specific energy of 142.1 Wh kg−1, at the specific power of 1350 W kg−1 with the downfall of the specific energy of 18.75 Wh kg−1 at the specific power of 67,500 W kg−1 with exceptional stability (79% of its initial capacitance retention). Additionally, we demonstrated a single AASC device with enough energy to light a 20 mW green LED and emitted light over 40 s, which opens up possible realistic applications. Finally, this study provides an avenue for fabricating and designing a wide voltage using a compositing technique, which seems to be one of the promising route to increase specific energy of AASCs.

ce:section.ce:para: To promote the accelerated production of portable electronic devices and vehicle backup power sources, high-performance energy storage devices must be developed [1–4]. The high specific power, quick charge/discharge speeds, high cyclic stability, and safety, aqueous supercapacitors (ASCs) have gotten much attention [5–8]. However, increasing the specific energy of AASCs without losing specific power or cycle durability remains a challenge. Several factors can increase the specific energy (E), such as improving the specific capacitance (C) or expanding the voltage window, rendering to the following eq. E = ½ C × V2 (V). A growing number of studies have focused on expanding their voltage windows to accompany the blockage of additiontoal enlightening the capacitive performance, with ultrahigh specific capacitances achieved by designing micro/nanostructure electrode materials [9,10]. However, the low voltage window of ASCs (∼1.23 V, theoretically) due to water decomposition [11,12], limits their potential use for practical applications. An established approach to enlarge the operating voltage window is constructing aqueous asymmetric supercapacitors (AASCs) with anodes and cathodes operating at various possible windows [13]. The electrochemical behavior of electrodes determines the overall performance of AASCs. This can be further achieved if extra overpotential for HER and OER are used, the operating voltage window of AASCs could be extended even further [14,15].

Furthermore, a SCs energy-storage performance can be determined by the electrode materials. Owing to their decent electrochemical properties, extraordinary stability, and cost-effectiveness, manganese dioxide (MnO2) is considered one of the promising pseudocapcitive material from a decades. From the electrochemical perspective, theoretical capacitance of MnO2 (∼1370 F g−1) due to fast redox reactions between Mn³⁺/Mn⁴⁺ ions. However, the experimental value is still under 300 F g−1, which impedes its applications in energy storage devices. Furthermore, the potential window of MnO2, according to the available literature, is approximately 1 V, considering E = ½ CV², thus limiting the power and specific energy to much lower than the theoretical value. To address these challenges, one-dimensional (1D), nanostructured MnO2, comprising nanorods, nanowires, nanofibers and nanotubes, are considered promising candidates because of their intriguing electrocatalytic properties, charge transport and large electroactive areas [16,17]. However, during long-term charge/discharge cycles, pure 1D-MnO2 nanostructures exhibit crystallographic instability, volume expansion, and aggregation, thus leading to short cycle life and low rate capability [18]. MnO2-based cathodes have been explored in possible windows of 0.8–1 V over the past decade [19–23]. Recent studies have demonstrated that the increased OER overpotential (above 1.3 V) and pseudocapacitance of MnO2 can be effectively increased through the intercalation/deintercalation of cations (Na+/K +) [24,25].

Electrode modification techniques that produce homogeneous monocomponent materials have low cycling stability (e.g., structure engineering). As a result, in AASCs, the rational design of composite electrodes has been attempted. To overcome these drawbacks, 1D-MnO2 has been coupled with other materials exhibiting high conductivity, such as carbon nanomaterials and metal chalcogenides [26,27]. In particular, transition metal sulfides such as NiCo2S4 (denoted as NCS) not only have higher specific capacitances and electrochemical activity but also has around 100 times the electrical conductivity than NiCo2O4. In comparison, the conductivity of NiCo2O4 is greater than that of cobalt oxides and nickel oxides [17,28]. In this study, we have synthesized NCS nanosheets coated over MnO2 that could serve as an ideal conductive scaffold for MnO2 by facilitating electron transport. NCS nanosheets with a large specific surface area can significantly increase electrode/electrolyte contact area while shortening diffusion paths. In addition, it raises the number of electroactive sites, resulting in enhanced performance of the active materials [29].

When used as anode or cathode materials, transition metal oxides (TMOs) are quite intriguing because of their diverse oxidation states, which allow for richer redox couples. In recent decades, significant progress has been made in developing different TMOs for SCs [30,31]. Fe2O3 is one of the promising, and widely used anode material for AASCs because of the following features: a) rich redox couples allow high pseudocapacitance over a broad voltage window; b) cost-effectiveness compared to commercial activated carbon [32]; c) and environmental compatibility [33]. Nevertheless, the performance of Fe2O3 anodes hinder their poor cycling stability, structural degradation lead to unsatisfactory capacitive performance. As a result of the structural instability and deprived electrical conductivity of the Fe2O3. The rGO aerogel is an ideal conductive substrate to construct Fe2O3 composite anode, which significantly boost the performance of the composite electrode [34–37]. Furthermore, rGO aerogel can effectively hinder the aggregation/restacking of graphene during composite fabrication and usage. As a result, the Fe2O3/rGO composite would be a more promising supercapacitor electrode material than pure Fe2O3 material.

Some researchers have widened the voltage window of SCs in an attempt to increase energy density. The voltage window (above 1.23 V, theoretically) has restricted the uses of aqueous electrolytes due to water decomposition [38]. Multiple methods have been used to expand the voltage window, including concentrated electrolytes [39,40], pH-adjusted electrolytes [41], and dual shuttle-ion electrolytes [42]. To be practical, each of these techniques requires sophisticated processing technology and sacrifices capacitance. Recently, Zhu and coworkers investigated the voltage induced by the structural alteration of Mn3O4 with Na+ ion intercalation/deintercalation, extending the potential up to 1.3 V [24]. The material structure design effectively undertakes the potential of one electrode, which is the most viable method to increase the working voltage. Theoretical studies based on first-principles calculations hypothesized that conductive materials could produce heterostructures with TMO via covalent bonding, which has a distinct voltage drop in the semiconductive area [43,44]. These studies provide a new design concept. Moreover, further research efforts should be directed toward developing better electrodes that increase potential without compromising specific capacitance.

In this paper, a hydrothermally prepared NCS/α-MnO2 and α-Fe2O3/rGO aerogel composite were employed as cathode and anode for the construction of high-performance AASC application for the first time. The heterostructured α-Fe2O3/rGO electrodes have rGO formed in situ, resulting a Fe-O-C covalent bonds are formed which is responsible for such lower voltage window, i.e., -1.5 to 0.1 V. The α-Fe2O3/rGO electrodes not only broaden the SCs operating voltage window to −1.5–0 V by lowering the voltage in the heterostructural region, resulting in an improved electrochemical performance of the anode material. Henceforth, the as-fabricated cathode (NCS/α-MnO2) when serve for SCs which displayed a high capacitance of (594 F g−1 and 270 F g−1@1–10 A g−1 in a three-electrode system. Finally, the assembled AASC device showed a high capacitance of 116 F g−1@1 A g−1, and a widen voltage window (2.7 V) with exceptionally high specific energy of 142.1 Wh kg−1 at a high specific power of 67,500 W kg−1 suppressing most of the previous AASCs.

XRD patterns were obtained first to confirm the formation and structure/phase clarity of the as-synthesized products. As revealed in Fig. 1
a, in which the peaks at around 2θ = 31.6°, 38.4°, and 55.4° can be indexed to the respective (311), (400), (440) planes of NCS (JCPDS 20–0782) [17,45]. However, some extra peaks were also observed, which are located at around 18.2°, 50.1°, 58.2° that can be allotted to (200), (141), and (600) planes of α-MnO2 with JCPDS 44–0141 [46]. Besides, no visible diffraction peaks of any other phases were visible, indicating the as-obtained composite's high purity. As shown from Fig. 1
b, the XRD pattern of α-Fe2O3/rGO aerogel composite displays the XRD reflections at around 24° corresponding to the (002) planes of rGO. Moreover, all other diffraction peaks are related to that of α-Fe2O3. Fig. 1
c is the crystal structure orientation of NCS/α-MnO2 while Fig. 1
d represents the structural growth of α-Fe2O3/rGO aerogel composite.

The chemical bonding and elemental composition of the NCS/α-MnO2 and α-Fe2O3/rGO composites were further examined by XPS study, as shown in Fig. 2
. Using the Gaussian fitting method, the Ni, Co, Mn, Fe, S, C are fitted with two spin-orbit doublets along with satellites (denoted as ‘‘Sat.’’). Fig. 2
a illustrates the Ni 2p spectrum, where prominent peaks centered at about 855.2 eV and 876.4 eV accredited to the Ni 2p3/2 and Ni 2p1/2. The resultant satellite peaks are located at around 861.2 eV and 883.7 eV, respectively, specifying the characteristics of Ni2+ and Ni3+. A similar result is also observed in the Co 2p spectrum, as presented in Fig. 2
b. The peaks at around 781.2 eV and 798.5 eV are apparent, that can be assigned to Co 2p3/2 and Co 2p1/2, with corresponding satellite peaks are situated at around 787.1 eV and 805.6 eV, respectively, specifying the co-existence of Co2+/Co3+ in the composites [47]. As displayed in Fig. 2
c, the S 2p spectrum contained peaks at 161.5 eV for S 2p3/2, attributed to the sulfur ion, while the peak at 162.6 eV for S 2p1/2 is a metal-sulfur bonds [48]. Fig. 2
d shows a high-resolution XPS spectrum of Mn 2p, where peaks centered at 646.2 eV and 654.1 eV are observed, corresponding to Mn's spin-orbit doublet states 2p3/2 and Mn 2p1/2 with a peak to peak separation of 11.8 eV, which is similar to the already reported literature [49,50]. The major peak centered at around 530.0 eV can be attributed to the Mn-O-Mn bonding, while the peaks at 531.5 eV and 532.1 eV can be respectively assigned to the Mn-O-H (-OH) and HO-H bonding (absorbed water) as shown in Fig. 2
e [51]. Based on the XPS results, the near-surface of NCS/α-MnO2 composite has chemical compositions of Ni3+, Ni2+, Co3+, Co2+, Mn3+, Mn4+, S2− and O, which is following the NCS/α-MnO2. Fig. 2
f and Fig. 2
g show the Fe 2p and O 1 s core-level spectra of the α-Fe2O3/rGO sample. The characteristic peaks 710.9 eV (for Fe 2p3/2) and 724.2 eV (for Fe 2p1/2) with a peak to peak separation of 13.2 eV [52]. Furthermore, O 1 s shows two main peaks at around 531.1 eV and 331.8 eV for O2- species confirms the co-existence of Fe2O3 on rGO aerogel composite. Likewise, C 1 s shows three characteristic peaks at around 284.6 eV, 285.2 eV, and 288.6 eV that are given to sp3 hybridized CC, CO, and O C = C, correspondingly [53].

Fig. 3 TEM images of NCS/ α-MnO2 and α-Fe2O3/rGO nanomaterials at various magnifications. Fig. 3
a, b shows the large-scale TEM images of NCS/α-MnO2 nanomaterials having nanosheets/rods-like morphology. The NCS nanosheets wrapped over α-MnO2 nanorods rise the surface area of the sample. For instance, the nanosheets/rods like morphology mainly increases the number of electroactive sites and provides efficient ion/electron transport to accommodate the volume variation and shorten the diffusion paths; as a result, suitable electrochemical activities of the electrode material was expected [54]. The inset in Fig. 3
b reveals the high-magnified image of α-MnO2 nanorod (about 20 nm) and several nanometers in length with a very uniform size. As can be seen from Fig. 3
c, a high-magnified image further confirm the successful growth of NCS nanosheets on α-MnO2 nanorods. Fig. 3
d shows a highly magnified image of the NCS nanosheets-like morphology of the product. The polycrystalline nature of the NCS/α-MnO2 composite is evident in the SAED pattern, as shown Fig. 3
d (inset). Fig. 3
e-g spectacles the TEM images of α-Fe2O3/rGO nanomaterials with smaller interconnected nanorods-like morphology of α-Fe2O3 uniformly on the surface of the rGO aerogel network. Fig. 3
g offers the composite's high-magnified image, proving the high purity of the interconnected nanorods sample. Fig. 3
h shows the product's high-magnified image with interlayer spacing also confirms the formation of by-product by a facile method on the rGO network. As shown in Fig. 3
i, the SAED pattern with diffraction spots demonstrates the product's polycrystalline nature. This novel nanostructure (grass/aerogel) dramatically reduces the mass transport resistances involved in ion diffusion and electrolyte penetration and eases the electron hopping between neighbouring particles [54].

For NCS/α-MnO2 and α-Fe2O3/rGO, N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and the resultant pore size distributions are shown in Fig. S1a, b. The surface areas of NCS/α-MnO2 and α-Fe2O3/rGO were 87.87 m2 g−1 and 10.03 m2 g−1, respectively. It was found that the average pore diameters of the NCS/α-MnO2 and for α-Fe2O3/rGO materials are in the range of 25 and 39 nm. The inclusion of NCS could significantly improve the porosity and specific surface area of the material, which was anticipated to increase the specific capacitance and vice versa.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Supercapacitive performance evaluation of α-Fe2O3/rGO and NCS/α-MnO2 electrodes (three-electrode system)
ce:section.ce:para:
The capacitive performance of α-Fe2O3/rGO (−1.5 to 0 V) and NCS/α-MnO2 (0 to 1.2 V) materials are assessed in a three-electrode configuration, with Na2SO4 as the electrolyte solution. However, after a few cycles, Na+ ions may occupy more sites at the electrode surface, limiting the recombination of nascent hydrogen to produce H2, thus improving the stability of negative potential −1.5 V owing to the synergistic effect of rGO and α-Fe2O3 in these two aspects. During the electrochemical procedure, the metal cations were first injected into the unique structural lattice. During electrochemical activities, in the α-Fe2O3 layers, the pre-inserted metal cations intercalated and taking advantage of the wide diffusion tunnel and close contact between two principal constituents. As a result, the intercalation of additional metal cations increased the OER overpotential. Second, the α-Fe2O3/rGO specific surface area provides extra space/volume for electrolyte ion transport, efficient synergizing utilization. Furthermore, the rGO framework can prevent the electrode from pulverizing throughout electrochemical process, resulting in excellent stability of the active electrode material. As illustrated in Fig. 4
a, b signifies the CV curves of α-Fe2O3/rGO, and NCS/α-MnO2 shows non-rectangular behavior with excellent symmetry at various scan rates, indicating a faradic redox reaction mechanism of the electrode materials. Even at scan rates as high as 400 mVs−1, both electrodes maintain comparable CV curves, indicating their outstanding rate capabilities. The GCD curves of α-Fe2O3/rGO and NCS/α-MnO2 electrodes are shown in Fig. 4
c, d, showing that NCS/α-MnO2 display a large discharge time compared to the former, exhibiting a high specific capacitance. The GCD profile has nearly perfect symmetry, indicating that the composite electrodes have excellent energy storage capability. Based on the charge/discharge measurements, the specific capacitance was calculated using eqn. (S-1) (supporting information) at various current densities and are shown in Fig. 4
e and maximum specific capacitance reaches 593.3 F g−1 (NCS/α-MnO2 electrode), and 270.0 F g−1 (α-Fe2O3/rGO electrode) @1 A g−1 and the values are tabulated in Table 1
. The specific capacitance values obtained are higher than those reported in several previous studies, such as NiCo2S4/Fe2O3 (342 F g−1 at 5 mVs−1)[52], NiCo2S4@MnO2 (520 F g−1 at 1 A g−1) [55], NiCo2S4@MnO2 (17.5 F cm−2 at 100 mA cm−2)[56], SiO2@C-NCS (625 F g−1 at 1 mA cm−2) [17], 3D NiCo2S4@MnO2 hybrid (2.6 F cm−2 at 3 mA cm−2) [57], α-Fe2O3/rGO composite (255 F g−1 at 0.5 A g−1) [58], α-Fe2O3/rGO nanocomposite (238 F g−1 at 5 mVs−1) [59]. The cycling stability of the electrode materials is another important parameter for commercial SC applications, as shown in Fig. 4
f. After 5000 cycles, the NCS/α-MnO2 electrode exhibits long-term cyclic stability, with capacitance retention of 99% at a current density of 6 A g−1, indicating exceptional active material stability. The high surface utilization of NCS/α-MnO2 accounts for the outstanding stability (
Fig. 4
f (red line). The high conductivity of NCS and its good adhesion to α-MnO2 are advantageous for effective ion and electron transport and greater volume variation with stable morphology. The α-Fe2O3/rGO electrode, on the other hand, was retained 123% after 5000 cycles, indicating that the electrode material has excellent cycling stability and energy storage performance. The 123% capacity retained after 5000 cycles can be attributed to the activation of the electrode material where the electrolyte is permeating into the α-Fe2O3/rGO electrode leading to an increase of electrode/electrolyte interface [60]. The increased capacitance with cycle number could be attributed to improved conductivity, allowing electrolyte ions to enter the active electrode/electrolyte interface more quickly.

To examine the practical application of the NCS/α-MnO2 and α-Fe2O3/rGO electrodes, a high-performance AASC device is assembled by integrating the NCS/α-MnO2 cathode with the α-Fe2O3/rGO anode. The CV curve potential window of the α-Fe2O3/rGO electrode (−1.5 to 0.0 V) differs from the NCS/α-MnO2 electrode (0.0 to 1.2 V). These electrodes have a perfect chance of being assembled in an AASC device with a wide working voltage (2.7 V), as shown in Fig. 5
a. To optimize performance, the positive and negative electrodes must maintain charge balance (supporting information, eq. S-2), and the mass ratio was calculated using eq. S-2 to be 0.57. (see section 1.5, supporting information).The CV curves were tested for a range of scan rates e.g., very low to very high (5 to 2000 mVs−1) to validate the AASC device target voltage window. The AASC device exhibits excellent reversibility by improving current response with prolonged sweep rates. When the scan rate is increased to 2000 mVs−1, the CV shapes were well maintained, as shown in Fig. 5
b, demonstrating the AASC device excellent reversibility and energy storage performance. Furthermore, the CV curves exhibit pseudocapacitive and double-layer capacitance modes at all sweep rates (5 to 2000 mVs−1), as presented in Fig. 5
b. The symmetrical GCD plots at densities ranging from 1 to 50 A g−1 will confirm good electrochemical activities and reversibility of the AASC device, as shown in Fig. 5
c. The specific capacitance of the AASC device was determined using the discharge curve, and the results are shown in Fig. 5
d. The maximum specific capacitance of the device is 140 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 and 18 F g−1 at 50 A g−1, with detailed values available in Table S1 (Supporting information). Owing to the solid interface in contact with the electrode surface, the specific capacitance decreases as charge/discharge rates increase (
Fig. 5
d). Another crucial factor is the stability of electrode material is an essential parameter for the practicality of electrode materials. The cycling stability of the AASC device after 10,000 charge/discharge cycles at 6 A g−1 is shown in Fig. 5
e. For example, only 21% of the original capacitance decline was detected from 1 to 1000 charge/discharge cycles (79% initial capacitance retention), demonstrating the outstanding stability of the AASC device. Meanwhile, over the first 1000 cycles, the specific capacitance steadily decreases due to morphological failure (structural degradation) of the active electrode material due to volume changes caused by the ion-insertion/desertion process [61].

The EIS plot, which describes the resistive and capacitive properties of electrodes, can be used to analyze the origins of the diverse actions of all electrodes ( Fig. 6
a). The straight-line realizes the pure capacitive action of electrodes. Due to the well-established interface between electrode and electrolyte ions, the AASC device has very low Rs (0.60 Ω) and Rct (0.63 Ω) before cycling. It reaches up to 1.08 Ω and 0.73 Ω values after cycling, indicating rapid ion transport during the charge/discharge process, establishing a robust electrochemical response. The bode plot shows a frequency-dependent phase angle, which differentiates the electrodes ability to charge storage (resistive or capacitive in nature). The phase angle (
Fig. 6
b) of an ideal electrochemical double-layer capacitor is 90°, while the phase angle of a pseudocapacitor varies [62]. The phase angles of the AASC device electrodes are almost identical. Still, the AASC device has a maximum phase angle of −76.56°and −63.02° before and after the cycling test, indicating suitable for manufacturing low leakage SCs. The exact equivalence τ0 = 1/f0 with a projected phase angle of 45° is also a significant factor of merit for SCs [63]. Furthermore, the AASC device displays a relaxation time constant of (1.81 s before the cycling test) and (2.82 s after the cycling test) shown by the bode plot induces rapid ion diffusion at the electrode-electrolyte interface (
Fig. 6
b).

Specific energy and power, which are critical factors for SC devices, are calculated using the capacitance values obtained. The discharge curves measured energy and power densities, then depicted in a Ragone plot (
Fig. 7
a, b). Surprisingly, the assembled AASC device provide ultrahigh specific energy of 142.1 W h kg−1 and a high specific power of 67,500 W kg−1 (
Fig. 7
a), significantly higher than other previously recorded AASCs, for instance, NiCo LDH/3D-Ni) anode and Mn3O4/3D-Ni hybrid as the cathode exhibits a high specific energy of (153.3 Wh kg−1 at 8810 W kg−1) [73], N-rGO//N-rGO (128 Wh kg−1 at 813 W kg−1) [64], Na0.91MnO2//carbon fiber fabric (138 Wh kg−1 at 3300 W kg−1) [65], CC/CW/p-VN@C//CC/CW/Na0.5MnO2 (96.7 Wh kg−1 at 1294 W kg−1) [66], Al2O3/Fe2O3@CNTs/Na2SO3//MnO2@CNTs (174 Wh kg−1 at 4492 W kg−1) [67], hrGO/rGO//Fe2O3 (91.1 Wh kg−1 at 24,008.1 W kg−1) [68], as listed in Table 2
. Fig. 7
b shows the enlarged view of the red encircled selected region in the Ragone plot of our assembled AASC device without literature data points, indicating ultra-high energy and specific power.

The excellent electrochemical properties of our assembled 2.7 V AASC device can be attributed to the smart cathode and anode designs, as seen schematically in Fig. 7 c. First, rGO is used as self-supporting active materials, which can significantly minimize “dead volume” and increase active material use, resulting in higher specific capacitance and excellent cycling stability. Second, the morphologies of α-Fe3O4 ultra-thin nanorods and NCS nanosheets endow the electrodes with a large surface area and a quick/favorable faradaic reaction in high power, high rate, and numerous pathways for fast charge transfer pathways during electrochemical activities. Third, the rGO aerogel protection layer improves conductivity and buffer volume shift when lowering HER activity. Finally, the AASC device has a large working voltage and ultra-high energy, and specific power due to the smart nature of the cathode and anode with wide and broad potential windows and matchable high specific capacity performance. Fourthly, the unique heterostructures between NCS and MnO2 and α-Fe3O4 and rGO increase the potential window to 0.0 V-1.2 V and −1.5 to 0.0 V and increase the specific capacitance of the electrode, potentially resulting in AASCs with ultrahigh specific energy. Finally, the outstanding electrochemical performance of the NCS/α-MnO2 and α-Fe2O3/rGO heterostructural electrodes is due to all of the above advantages. As a result of these exceptional electrochemical properties, the AASC device is in a position to become a leading contender for electrochemical energy storage and conversion devices shortly.

Scheme 1
a, b depicts the energy band diagrame to understand the in-depth relationship between the increase in capacitance and the expansion of the potential window with the heterostructure of α-Fe2O3. In general, due to their different work functions at the interface of two materials a Schottky electric field is established [60,74,75]. For example, the work functions of α-Fe2O3 and rGO in α-Fe2O3/rGO are 6.10–5.49 and 5.2 eV [76,77]; free electrons can flow from rGO to α-Fe2O3 at the interface till the Fermi levels are line up (
Scheme 1
a). Then, under equilibrium, the rGO is positively charged while α-Fe2O3 is negatively charge due to the injection of free electrons into the α-Fe2O3 from rGO near its surface. Additionally, owing to the limited lateral scale of α-Fe2O3, the built-in electric field cannot be completely screened. This causes the free electrons to be accumulated near the α-Fe2O3 surface, as schematically shown in Scheme 1
b. Furthermore, the zigzag edges of the special α-Fe2O3 allow rGO to inject free electrons at the boundaries. Apparently, allowed electrons accumulated at the boundaries of α-Fe2O3 with sodium ions may cause extra electrostatic attraction, resulting in a synergistic effect that increased caapcitance further. Nonetheless, the lower energy bandgap, only a few electrons can inject from rGO to α-Fe2O3 at the interface before the Fermi levels are aligned [78,79].

Our α-Fe2O3/rGO heterostructural materials have a potential window of −1.5 V. According to the findings mentioned above; there are two leading causes for the expansion of the potential window: the first is the presence of rGO, and the second is the existence of heterostructures between rGO and α-Fe2O3 (
Fig. 1
d, and
Scheme 1
c). Hydrothermal synthesis of α-Fe2O3/rGO composite electrodes, on the other hand, cannot increase the volatge further than 1 V, indicating that the distinctive heterostructure has the most significant impact on expanding the potential window. The design of covalent bonds in the interface bonding would enhance the production of heterostructures, as previously described [60,80], suggesting that only rGO and α-Fe2O3 are strongly linked via Fe-O-C bonds can build trustworthy heterostructures. The valence and conduction bands of rGO and α-Fe2O3 also bend in the vicinity of the interface, resulting in the formation of a potential gradient at the interface [79], implying that an electric field in the α-Fe2O3/rGO heterostructural region acts as a potential barrier to electronic transmission. To widen the potential window for SCs, this barrier may perform partial potential and counteract external electric fields. Consequently, we investigate novel types of electrodes for high-specific energy and high-performance SCs that may expand the potential window without sacrificing specific capacitance. Therefore, the industrial implementation benefit of this AASC device can also be checked by the LED light illuminated for more than 40 s, as shown in scheme 1
d-f. We demonstrated a single AASC device with enough energy to turn on a 20 mW green LED and emit light over time to open up possible realistic applications. NCS/α-MnO2//α-Fe2O3/rGO based AASC devices could be a promising candidate for modern electronic devices.

The current study offerings a hierarchical α-Fe2O3 ultra-thin nanowires growth on rGO aerogel (α-Fe2O3/rGO) composite as the anode. Remarkably, the as-prepared α-Fe2O3/rGO composite anode voltage can be effectively extended up to −1.5 to 0 V due the additional OER, which significantly enhanced specific capacitance (270 F g−1@ 1 A g−1), excellent cyclic performance. On the other side, a NCS/α-MnO2 cathode with similar outstanding capacitive performance has been prepared in the present work. Interestingly, due to well-separated working voltage ranges and desirable specific capacity of both electrodes, the as-fabricated NCS/α-MnO2//α-Fe2O3/rGO AASC device operate stably in a wide voltage range of 2.7 V that can effectively enhanced a specific capacitance of the AASC device up to 140 F g−1@1 A g−1, with ultra-high specific energy of 142.1 W h kg−1, at the specific power of 1350 W kg−1, outstanding cycling stability, which is higher than most of the previous reports based on AASCs. Additionally, we demonstrated a single AASC device with 20 mW green LED could be successfully light over 40 s, opening possible realistic applications. As a result, this study provides a foundation for developing compositing strategies that efficiently increase the working voltage and specific energy of AASCs.
Muhammad Sajjad: Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Resources, Conceptualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Wen Lu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no conflicts of interest in influencing the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: There is no funding source is available to be acknowledged
